summary: The initiative delivers an offline, single‑HTML‑file Sudoku web game for casual puzzle players. It provides a responsive 9x9 grid with three difficulty levels, core gameplay features (timer, mistake counter, pause/resume) and a completion screen, and runs fully in the browser without network requests.

assumptions.text: Assume all puzzle data will be embedded in the HTML file as a static JavaScript array, since no external storage is mentioned.
Assume the UI will be built with plain HTML/CSS/JS without third‑party UI frameworks, due to the single‑file constraint.
Assume the timer, mistake counter, and pause functionality are client‑side only with no persistence across page reloads.
Assume keyboard input is required only for desktop browsers; mobile devices will use on‑screen controls.
Assume no accessibility features (e.g., screen‑reader support) are required at this stage.

requirementsInterpreted.text: Load a new puzzle of the selected difficulty (easy, medium, hard) from a predefined set of valid boards.
Render a 9x9 Sudoku grid showing fixed starting numbers and editable cells.
Prevent users from editing the fixed starting numbers while allowing edits to other cells.
Allow users to select a cell and enter numbers 1‑9 via keyboard (desktop) or on‑screen controls (mobile).
Highlight the selected cell’s row, column, and 3x3 box, and optionally indicate duplicate numbers or conflicts.
Provide controls to reset the current puzzle or start a new puzzle.
Detect when the board is completely and correctly solved and show a completion overlay with time, difficulty, and mistake count.
Show a running timer, mistake counter, and a pause/resume button during gameplay.

outOfScope.text: Persistent user accounts, cloud save, or any server‑side storage.
Integration with external APIs or services for puzzle fetching or analytics.
Advanced hint or solving assistance beyond basic validation.
Use of heavy UI frameworks or external libraries.

riskFlags.text: Single‑file offline constraint limits ability to fetch external puzzles, requiring all data to be embedded.
Prohibiting external libraries may increase development effort to achieve a polished UI.
Potential lack of automated test coverage for the game engine could lead to undetected edge‑case bugs.
